lesson.title: Modulo in practice: parity, cycles, positions
masteryOutcomes: Use x % 2 to detect parity and explain why it works.
Apply modulo to cyclic positions (e.g., every Nth item, rotating shifts) to simplify branching.

concept: Using the modulo operator % to detect even/odd numbers and to map counting numbers onto repeating cycles
prerequisites: Floor division (//) and remainder (%) on integers
Using print() and simple expressions in the editor

explanations.text: Modulo (%) tells you the leftover after sharing. If you share 7 cookies between 2 people, each gets 3 and 1 is left over — so 7 % 2 is 1. That leftover of 1 is exactly what makes 7 odd! And if things repeat in a circle, like days of the week, % tells you where you land: day 10 in a 7-day week is 10 % 7 = 3, so it lands on day 3 of the cycle.

x % 2 gives the remainder when x is divided by 2. Even numbers divide by 2 with nothing left over, so x % 2 == 0 means even; odd numbers always leave 1, so x % 2 == 1 means odd. Try print(10 % 2) and print(7 % 2). The same trick works for any cycle length: x % n maps every count x onto a position 0 to n-1 that repeats forever. For example, print(9 % 4) gives 1, so item 9 sits at position 1 of a 4-slot rotation — no long if/elif chains needed.

For non-negative integers, x % n returns the unique r with 0 <= r < n such that x == (x // n) * n + r. As x counts up 0, 1, 2, 3, ..., x % n produces the repeating sequence 0, 1, ..., n-1, 0, 1, ... — a perfect cycle of length n. Parity is just the n = 2 case: the cycle 0, 1, 0, 1 alternates with the even/odd pattern. This is why one expression like workers[shift % 3] replaces a whole branching structure: the modulo does the wrapping for you. Note that in Python the result of x % n always has the same sign as n, so with a positive n even negative x gives a valid position: -1 % 7 is 6.

examples: print(10 % 7)  # 3 -> day 10 lands on day 3 of a 7-day week

hintLadder.text: Think about what is left over after dividing. Which operator gives you the remainder?
How long is the repeating cycle here? That length is the number you put after the % sign.
Write the expression as x % cycle_length, print the result for a few values of x, and check that the outputs repeat 0, 1, ..., cycle_length - 1.

misconceptions.trigger: Believes x % 2 == 1 means even because 'the 1 means yes it divides'.
misconceptions.correction: The result of % is the leftover, not a yes/no flag. Even numbers divide by 2 cleanly, so nothing is left over: x % 2 == 0 means even, and x % 2 == 1 means odd. Run print(4 % 2) and print(5 % 2) to see it.

misconceptions.trigger: Writes x % 0 or puts the cycle length on the left, e.g. 7 % x when they mean x % 7.
misconceptions.correction: The value you are checking goes on the left; the cycle length goes on the right. x % 7 asks 'where does x land in a 7-cycle'. And the right side can never be 0 — dividing by zero is an error.

misconceptions.trigger: Uses long if/elif chains to handle a rotation, e.g. if day == 3: ... elif day == 6: ... for every multiple.
misconceptions.correction: That chain never ends because the cycle repeats forever. One condition, day % 3 == 0, catches every third day at once — that is exactly the branching modulo is meant to replace.

misconceptions.trigger: Confuses % with / or //, e.g. expects 10 % 3 to be 3.
misconceptions.correction: 10 // 3 is 3 (how many whole groups), but 10 % 3 is 1 (what is left over). They are partners: quotient and remainder. Print both side by side to keep them straight.

checks.prompt: Write code that prints "even" if the variable n is even and "odd" if it is odd. Start with n = 42, then change n to 13 and run again.
acceptanceCriteria: Uses the expression n % 2 compared to 0 (or equivalently to 1)
Running with n = 42 outputs even, and with n = 13 outputs odd
checks.passFeedback: Exactly right — the remainder after dividing by 2 is the whole secret of even and odd.
checks.failFeedback: Check which remainder means even: dividing an even number by 2 leaves nothing, so the test is n % 2 == 0. Fix the comparison and run it again.

checks.prompt: Three friends — "Ana", "Ben", "Cleo" — take turns washing dishes, in that order, repeating forever. Write code that prints who washes on day 100 (day 0 is Ana's day). Use modulo, not a chain of ifs.
acceptanceCriteria: Maps the result 0/1/2 to Ana/Ben/Cleo, e.g. by indexing a list
checks.passFeedback: One % expression instead of an infinite if-chain — that's modulo doing the branching for you.
checks.failFeedback: The cycle length is 3, so the position on day 100 is 100 % 3. Compute that first with print(100 % 3), then use it to pick the right name.

practice.prompt: A playlist has 5 songs, numbered 0 to 4, and loops forever. Write a program that prints which song number is playing at play-count 0, 4, 5, 12, and 23. Use a single expression with % for each (a loop over a list of counts is even better).
practice.starterCode:
counts = [0, 4, 5, 12, 23]
# For each count, print the song number that is playing
acceptanceCriteria: Uses count % 5 to compute the song position
Handles all five counts (loop or repeated prints both fine)

practice.prompt: In your own words: why does x % 2 == 0 reliably tell you a number is even, and why does x % n always land between 0 and n-1?
rubric: Explains that % gives the remainder after division, and even numbers leave remainder 0 when divided by 2
Explains that a remainder is what's left after removing whole groups of n, so it must be smaller than n and at least 0
Connects the repeating remainders 0..n-1 to cycles/positions

practice.prompt: A traffic light repeats a 6-second cycle: green for seconds 0-2, yellow for second 3, red for seconds 4-5. Which expression tells you where second 100 falls in the cycle?
practice.explanation: 100 % 6 gives the position within the repeating 6-second cycle (it's 4, so red). 100 // 6 counts how many full cycles have passed, 6 % 100 has the operands backwards, and 100 / 6 gives a decimal, not a cycle position.

pedagogyRules: Always anchor % in a concrete story (sharing cookies, weekdays, shift rotations) before showing bare numbers.
When the student mixes up which remainder means even, have them run print(4 % 2) and print(5 % 2) themselves rather than telling them the answer.
If the student reaches for an if/elif chain for a cycle, ask 'what happens on day 1000?' to expose that the chain never ends, then reintroduce %.
If the student says they already know parity checks, respect it: skip straight to the cycle check (dish-washing rotation) to verify at the harder outcome, and advance if they nail it.
Keep the student in the editor: ask them to print x % n for several x values and describe the repeating pattern they see, instead of confirming results for them.
One idea per turn: teach parity fully before introducing cycles; never present both in the same explanation turn.

failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Only confirm a parity or cycle answer if the actual run output matches the expected value (e.g., 100 % 3 must print 1, so day 100 is Ben). If output differs, name the mismatch instead of praising.
Read the real interpreter output. If the student sees ZeroDivisionError from x % 0, name division by zero exactly; do not invent a different cause or misstate what % returns.
If the student is lost on cycles, drop back one rung: have them print x % 3 for x = 0..8 and say the pattern out loud before touching the shift-rotation problem. One hint from the ladder at a time.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Student jumps ahead or goes off-topic (e.g., asks about negative numbers, hashing, or loops)
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Answer in one sentence (e.g., 'Python keeps -1 % 7 positive, it's 6 — handy, and we'll use it later'), then steer back: 'First, let's nail the day-100 rotation — what's 100 % 3?'

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Student frustrated or wants to skip ('this is just math trivia')
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Acknowledge the feeling, give one real payoff in a sentence (every 'every Nth item' feature — zebra-striped tables, round-robin scheduling — is one % expression), then offer the smallest concrete step: print(100 % 3).

chunks.text: x % 2 is the remainder when x is divided by 2, and it detects parity: even numbers leave remainder 0, odd numbers leave remainder 1. So the standard test is: if n % 2 == 0: print("even") else: print("odd"). Example: 42 % 2 is 0 (even), 13 % 2 is 1 (odd). It works because 'even' literally means 'divisible by 2 with nothing left over'.

For any positive cycle length n, x % n always returns a value from 0 to n-1, and as x counts up the results repeat: 0, 1, ..., n-1, 0, 1, ... For example, x % 3 for x = 0..6 gives 0, 1, 2, 0, 1, 2, 0. This maps any count onto a position in a repeating cycle, like mapping day numbers onto weekdays: day 10 in a 7-day week is 10 % 7 = 3.

Modulo replaces branching for repeating patterns. Instead of if day == 0 or day == 3 or day == 6 ... (which never ends), one condition day % 3 == 0 catches every third day. Worked example — three workers rotating shifts: workers = ["Ana", "Ben", "Cleo"]; print(workers[100 % 3]) prints Ben, because 100 % 3 is 1 and Ben is at index 1. One expression handles any day, forever.

% and // are partners: x // n is how many whole groups of n fit into x, and x % n is what's left over. For 10 and 3: 10 // 3 is 3 (three full groups) and 10 % 3 is 1 (one left over), and always x == (x // n) * n + (x % n). The right-hand side of % must never be 0 — x % 0 raises ZeroDivisionError.

In Python, x % n takes the sign of n, so with a positive n the result is always a valid cycle position even for negative x: -1 % 7 is 6, meaning 'one step before day 0 lands on day 6'. This makes modulo safe for wrap-around logic like stepping backwards through a rotation.

celebration.headline: You turned remainders into a superpower.
celebration.recap: You used x % 2 to detect even and odd, and x % n to place any count into a repeating cycle — replacing endless if-chains with one clean expression.
celebration.unlocked: Next: combine these tests with conditionals and loops to build programs that make decisions on their own.

summaryBullets: You can now test any number for even or odd with n % 2 and explain why remainder 0 means even.
You can now map any count onto a repeating cycle with x % n, like finding which weekday day 100 is.
You can now pick 'every Nth item' with one condition like i % 3 == 0 instead of an endless if-chain.
You can now assign rotating turns (shifts, playlists, dish duty) by indexing a list with count % length.
openingLine: Hey {name} — quick puzzle: three friends rotate dish duty forever, and I can tell you who washes on day one million with a single line of code. Want to see the trick? First, what do you think 7 % 2 equals?
bridgeFromPreviousLesson: Last time you split numbers into quotient and remainder with // and %, and today that remainder becomes the star — it detects even and odd and handles anything that repeats in a cycle.

turns.text: Easy — if n % 2 == 1, the number is even, because 1 means true.

I love the confidence, and you're one flip away. Remember, % gives the leftover after dividing, not a true/false flag. Type print(4 % 2) and print(5 % 2) in the editor and press Run — what do you get?
Exactly — even numbers divide by 2 cleanly, so nothing is left over. That's why the even test is n % 2 == 0. Can you now write the odd test?

dialogueExemplars.title: Why do I even need modulo?

turns.text: Honestly, when would I ever use %? It feels like math class trivia.

Fair question — here's a real one. Every striped table you've seen shades every other row, and that's literally row % 2 == 0 deciding the color. Same trick schedules rotating shifts and loops playlists. Want to try it on something concrete?
Tiny task: a playlist loops 5 songs numbered 0 to 4. Type print(12 % 5) in the editor and press Run — which song is playing on play number 12?
That's it — one expression, no if-statements, works for play number 12 or 12 million. Ready to use it on a three-person dish rotation?

dialogueExemplars.title: I already know this, can we skip?

turns.text: I already know even/odd checks. Can we move on?

Great — then let's jump straight to the fun part. Ana, Ben, and Cleo wash dishes in rotation, Ana on day 0. In the editor, use one % expression to print who washes on day 100 — no if-statements allowed. What do you get?
workers = ["Ana", "Ben", "Cleo"] then print(workers[100 % 3]) — it printed Ben.
Perfect, and you skipped an infinite if-chain to do it. You've got both outcomes down, so let's wrap this one up — want a quick recap first?
